mechanism_narrative: Declining federal funds rate directly reduces the discount rate applied to future corporate cash flows, increasing present value of equity claims. A sustained downward trend in FEDFUNDS over the preceding 3 months predicts positive S&P 500 returns over the next 20 trading days through mechanical valuation expansion.
evidence_refs: fact:FEDFUNDS at 2.0% as of 2008-06-01 reflects ongoing easing from prior peaks
failure_condition: Walk-forward Rank IC < 0.03 over any 2-year rolling window, or mean 20-day forward returns during periods of declining FEDFUNDS do not exceed baseline by at least 150 basis points (t-test p > 0.05).

mechanism_narrative: Rising unemployment rate signals deteriorating labor market conditions and reduced consumer spending capacity, which compresses corporate earnings expectations and produces negative S&P 500 forward returns. The mechanism operates through reduced aggregate demand and margin compression.
candidate_trigger: UNRATE rises > 0.4 percentage points over preceding 2 months
evidence_refs: fact:UNRATE at 5.5% as of 2008-06-01 shows early labor market deterioration
failure_condition: SPX produces positive 60-day forward returns in > 55% of triggered instances despite rising unemployment signal, or Rank IC < 0.02 over any 3-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: Declining housing starts (HOUST) signal weakening residential investment and forward-looking consumer confidence. This demand contraction propagates through construction employment and durable goods orders, producing negative equity returns over the next 5 trading days as market reprices growth expectations downward.
evidence_refs: fact:HOUST at 975k units (May 2008) reflects sharp housing market deterioration
failure_condition: Negative 5-day returns occur in < 50% of triggered instances, or correlation between HOUST changes and 5-day SPX returns is not significantly negative (p > 0.10).

mechanism_narrative: Industrial production growth reflects real economic capacity utilization and forward earnings generation. Declining INDPRO signals supply-side weakness and reduced profit margins. When INDPRO growth turns negative, equity valuations compress as investors reprrice earnings sustainability over the next 20 trading days.
evidence_refs: fact:INDPRO at 110.9 (May 2008) shows early supply contraction signals
failure_condition: Mean 20-day forward returns during INDPRO decline periods do not differ significantly from baseline (t-test p > 0.10), or Rank IC < 0.02 over any 2-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: Retail sales (RSAFS) and housing starts (HOUST) jointly capture consumer and residential demand. When both decline simultaneously, the signal indicates broad-based demand destruction that compresses earnings across consumer discretionary and construction-linked sectors. This interaction produces stronger negative returns than either signal alone.
candidate_trigger: RSAFS declines > 2% AND HOUST declines > 5% over preceding 2 months
evidence_refs: fact:Both RSAFS (384.2B) and HOUST (975k) show weakness as of mid-2008
failure_condition: Interaction coefficient (RSAFS × HOUST) is not statistically significant (p > 0.10) in predicting 20-day returns, or the joint signal produces worse predictive performance than either signal independently.

mechanism_narrative: Payroll employment (PAYEMS) and unemployment rate (UNRATE) jointly characterize labor market momentum. When PAYEMS declines while UNRATE rises simultaneously, the signal indicates accelerating labor market deterioration that triggers equity sell-offs as investors reprice recession probability and earnings risk over the next 60 trading days.
candidate_trigger: PAYEMS declines > 0.3% AND UNRATE rises > 0.3pp over preceding 3 months
evidence_refs: fact:PAYEMS at 137.6M and UNRATE at 5.5% show early labor market stress
failure_condition: Interaction term (PAYEMS × UNRATE) does not improve predictive power over additive model (R² improvement < 0.01), or 60-day forward returns are not significantly negative during joint deterioration periods (t-test p > 0.10).

mechanism_narrative: Inflation measured by CPIAUCSL constrains monetary policy easing capacity. When inflation remains elevated (CPI > 215 index points), the Federal Reserve cannot cut rates aggressively, limiting the valuation support available to equities. This regime-conditional mechanism predicts negative returns in high-inflation easing cycles versus positive returns in low-inflation easing cycles.
evidence_refs: fact:CPIAUCSL at 215.1 (May 2008) constrains Fed easing despite rate cuts
failure_condition: 20-day forward returns in high-inflation easing regimes are not significantly lower than low-inflation easing regimes (t-test p > 0.10), or the interaction coefficient is not statistically significant (p > 0.10).

mechanism_narrative: Real GDP growth (GDPC1) represents the broadest measure of economic demand and earnings capacity. In late-cycle regimes where GDPC1 growth has decelerated below 2% annualized, further slowdowns trigger equity repricing as investors shift from expansion to stagnation expectations. This regime-conditional mechanism predicts negative 60-day returns only when growth is already weak.
candidate_trigger: GDPC1 growth decelerates to < 1.5% annualized in late-cycle regime (UNRATE > 5%)
evidence_refs: fact:GDPC1 at 11703.6B (Q1 2008) shows early deceleration; UNRATE at 5.5% confirms late-cycle positioning
failure_condition: 60-day forward returns during late-cycle GDPC1 deceleration periods are not significantly negative (t-test p > 0.10), or the mechanism shows no regime-dependence (performance is identical in early-cycle vs. late-cycle regimes, p > 0.15).
